text:
This is a piece of code from a 
fully functional 8-bit snake game,

featuring razor-sharp graphics, 
mesmerizing gameplay, and even

sound! The reason that the code looks like 
this is because it's written in Brainf-.

I don't want to get demonetized, so I'll 
abbreviate the name to just BF. As you

can probably tell by the name, this language is 
quite literally designed to give you a headache,

I'm just one of the weirdos that 
finds it fun to work with - remember,

you're currently looking 
at a game engine. Point is,

if you don't take notes on every little thing 
your code does, it's very easy to get lost.

All programs written in BF look like 
unreadable garbage - it's not just my code,

I promise. That's because the entire BF grammar 
consists of just 8 symbols. Other characters,

like letters and even numbers, are 
simply ignored. But, in theory,

this is all you need to write any program - 
including a relatively complicated 2D game.

Here's how it works: you're given a long array 
of numbers, which are all set to 0 by default.

You've also got a cursor, which starts off 
pointing at the very first number. You can

use these two instructions to move that cursor to 
the right, or to the left. You can use the plus

and minus symbols to add 1 or remove 1 from the 
currently selected number. The comma lets you read

user input one symbol at a time, and the dot lets 
you output a symbol. Finally, the square brackets

indicate a loop - any code inside of here will 
repeat until the selected value becomes zero.

And yep, that's all there is to it. 
Congratulations, you've successfully

learned an entire programming language. Feel 
free to put that on your resume if you want,

though... maybe leave out the 
name. It's not very professional.

Okay, so we've got the LEGOs, but how 
do you build a game with this? For that,

we'll first need a screen. I wrote a program 
that visualizes the first 256 cells as pixels.

This means that if we, for example, 
increase the value of the first cell,

the first pixel will get brighter. If 
we do the same a few cells forward,

we can light up a different pixel. Slowly, little 
by little, we can start to build a whole image.

Technically, this screen doesn't have to be 
16 by 16, but that does make it easier to work

with when building a game. BF works with 8-bit 
values, which means that the highest number you

can achieve is 255. Going over that will just 
flip the value back over to 0. And, likewise,

subtracting one from zero will bring you up to 
255. A 16 by 16 pixel screen has exactly 256

pixels, which means that we can store something 
like the player position in just one cell.

Actually drawing a player based on this 
position is where it starts to get interesting.
Say we're storing the position in the 256th cell,

right outside of the pixel buffer. To 
draw the player at the given location,

we need a way to convert this number to a set 
of cursor moving instructions. Just hard-coding

these isn't gonna work, because then you'd 
have to change the code to move the player.

My solution to this was to use what I call 
a glider - it's a loop with just one arrow

instruction inside, and what this does is 
it makes the cursor continuously move in

one direction until it reaches a 0, 
at which point, the loop will stop.

That actually reframes the problem, as now 
we just need to fill some continuous volume

of cells with positive values, which is 
a lot more plausible than jumping ahead.

The code I'm using here just decrements the 
position counter, then glides into the pixel

buffer, increments one value at the end of the 
chain, and glides right back. Rinse repeat,

and you've got yourself a bunch of ones leading 
up to where the player is supposed to be. Now,

if you just use another set of gliders, 
you can go to the end of this chain,

draw a fully white pixel there, and then 
glide back while picking up your garbage

along the way. Yeah, this is not what 
game programming normally looks like.

At this point, you might say: “What's the big 
deal? We were already drawing pixels ages ago!”

But here's the cool part - we can now put all 
of the drawing code in its own loop, and make

changes to the position each time the loop runs, 
which makes that pixel start moving! It's not

quite Snake just yet, but we're getting there. 
What we need now is a way to control the player.

I mentioned earlier that the comma operator 
can read symbols from user input, but in my

engine I've slightly tweaked it to give me 
a bitmask of controller inputs. Simply put,

when I use the comma, I'll get back a 
number that can correspond to a button,

or a combination of buttons. To be clear - I'm 
not adding anything new, this is all still doable

in vanilla BF. I'm effectively just remapping 
these inputs to be more intuitive for a game.

So with that, and a bunch more BF code, I 
can finally start to move around a pixel

on the screen. And, depending on how easily 
entertained you are, one could already call

this a game. Maybe. The fundamental concept that 
makes this (and the rest of the game) possible,

are conditionals. In most programming languages, 
these are expressions like “is X equal to 5?” or

“is A less than B?”, but ohh no no no no- 
this is BF, we don't get such fancies. We

just have a few increments and loops. 
Still, undeterred, we can make it work.
If you think about it, the BF loop is kind of like

a conditional that asks “is the 
number not equal to zero?” If so,

it'll run and repeat the code that's inside. 
If not, it'll jump forward and skip the loop.

Now, that might seem like a dead end at first, but 
just watch: if we add a few subtractions before

the loop, we can now start to ask questions like 
“is the number not equal to 3?”, because this is

basically the same as carrying the number to the 
other side of the equation. We can also nest loops

inside of each other to ask questions like “is the 
number not 3 or 4?”. And while it might seem like

we're limited to just the “not equal” kinds of 
questions, there's a way to get around that, too!

Say we have a loop that always works - it has a 
dedicated flag number that we'll set to a non-

zero value. No matter the number comes before it, 
it'll always pass, because it's actually checking

a completely different number. But, remember 
- we've got the power to exclude any number,

so we can add another loop before this that 
checks if the cursor is not pointing towards a 3,

and if so, it'll flip that flag, which will now 
skip the second loop for anything that isn't a 3.
This might be a lot to take in all at once,

but essentially this means that we can 
start gluing together basic conditions

like “is the left direction key pressed, 
and if so, change the player's position”.

But, let's be honest, it's not 
really a game without an objective,

so let's try adding another slightly 
dimmer pixel – in Snake, this would be

the apple. But... that's kinda stupid, 
since snakes don't actually eat apples,

so just between us, the protagonist of this 
game will actually be a very hungry caterpillar.

Anyway, if we just copy the player rendering code, 
it seems to work at first, until you step to the

right of it. This happens because of the way 
we're gliding across the screen. In a situation

like this, The player gets drawn first, and then 
the apple's rendering routine has to glide over

it to be drawn after. But the apple can't really 
distinguish between a marker it placed and a pixel

that was already there, so the player effectively 
offsets the apple one cell to the left.

I don't know if there's a good way to solve 
this, at least I haven't figured it out yet,

and if I ever do, it's not like I'm going 
to come back and update my rendering

pipeline. It's not exactly trivial 
to refactor a program written in BF.

My solution is pretty dumb, but it does work 
- I just check if the player's coordinate is

less than than the apple's coordinate, 
and subtract one if so. The reason this

approach sucks is that you now have to 
do this for every new pixel you add,

making the code more and more of a brainfu- 
*ahem*, sorry, excuse me- almost got me there.

Lastly, we need a way to randomize the position 
of the apple after it's been picked up. BF

doesn't have any randomization functions 
per se, but just like many great NES games,

we can simulate randomness by using 
a timer that counts up every frame.

My engine runs this game at 60 FPS, and 
it'd be pretty hard to predict the exact

value of that counter every time I pick 
up an apple. So, it's basically random.

Speaking of frame rate, it's actually pretty 
hard to control the player with how fast it

moves at 60 FPS. I didn't mention this before, but 
I repurposed the dot operator that usually prints

a single character to instead signal to my runtime 
that the frame buffer is ready for drawing.

We could slow down the game by drawing 
multiple identical frames back to back,

but that would make movement feel 
much less responsive. Instead,

I'll increment a counter 32 times each frame, 
and only update the player's position once that

counter wraps around to zero, which 
would be every 8th frame. This way,

I can still read player input on every frame, 
but I process it at a much slower rate.

Okay, so we've got a pretty 
awesome dot collection game,

but this ain't no Snake. 
Where's the tail! Where's the-
yeah no, just... just, where's the tail?

Well, drawing one piece of the tail 
is about the same as drawing another

player that's just lagging behind by one 
tick. Making it longer is what's annoying,

and I actually procrastinated on this so hard 
that I ended up implementing 8-bit color.

This wasn't actually that hard - instead 
of drawing each pixel as a brightness,

I treat the numbers in the frame buffer as RGB332 
color values. This basically means that certain

numbers correspond to certain colors. So all 
I have to do to make the player green and the

apple red is to just use those specific numbers 
when drawing the pixels. To change the color of

the background, I can just add some number 
to every pixel in the frame buffer. That

does also mess up the colors of existing 
pixels, but this can be compensated for.

Okay, enough of that, we do actually need a 
tail. There are two ways to go about this - we

can either write a template for drawing one pixel 
of the tail and copy-paste that a bunch of times,

or we can figure out how to implement arrays. 
And, regardless of which option I'd choose,

because of my crappy rendering 
pipeline, each tail piece would

have to account for the position of every 
other tail piece to be ordered correctly.

The elegant solution here, if you can 
call it that, is to use some kind of

array structure. Remember- we can't tell 
BF to get the N-th tail piece directly.

We already solved a similar problem 
earlier with the frame buffer,

but that was for writing to an 
empty canvas - this is the opposite.

One way to do this is to free up 
twice the memory you would need,

and fill the second half with the coordinates. 
To retrieve an element from a specific index,

you can shift the entire array backwards by 
that amount, and then go to where the first

coordinate was - that will now hold your 
desired array element. This would be one

of the more elegant and beautiful and dynamic 
way to do things, but my code doesn't do this.

I just wrote some JavaScript code that 
takes the template for drawing one tail

piece and copy-pastes it 16 times. 
That's the dumb and stinky approach,

but the one benefit it does have is 
that it's actually quite a bit faster,

especially because you'd otherwise have to 
shift the whole array a bajillion times to

cross-compare all the tail pixel coordinates. 
And this might actually matter, as even with

the cheaper approach, my caterpillar starts to 
not feel so good after a few too many apples.

The solution to this is to just optimize the 
interpreter. For example, instead of executing

individual plus instructions one-by-one, 
you can count them up ahead of time and

store the exact amount to add. Likewise, 
instead of stepping all the way through a

loop to find the beginning or end, we can 
count the distance between brackets ahead

of time. This is actually how some basic 
compression systems work in the real world.

With just a few changes like these, 
there's no longer any bit of lag remaining,

even at full tail length. You could optimize 
this even further by converting this binary

format into C code, and then compiling 
that down to actual machine code that

runs natively on your processor, but 
I don't think our game will need that.

Implementing a game-over state for biting the 
tail is probably the easiest thing in this whole

project – all you have to do is create an infinite 
loop that draws the same frame over and over.

Now, I'd love to say that we're finally done 
with the game, but here comes the dreaded part:
debugging BF code.

There are quite a few graphical bugs, and even 
more gameplay bugs. Just to name an example,

if you move diagonally, 
you'll start skipping pixels,

which basically lets you go through 
your own tail if you get lucky.

I fixed that last one by making 
diagonal movement stepped. Basically,

I've got a value that flip-flops between 
zero and non-zero every time movement is

processed. Then, instead of applying both 
coordinate changes for diagonal movement,

I choose a direction based on that flag. This also 
ends up looking a lot more like what you'd expect.

Since I also can't make the tail infinitely 
long, you can exploit the way the screen

coordinates wrap around to do this, which is kind 
of like an infinite score glitch. To remedy that,

I added this blue pixel that kills you on contact. 
You can think of it as a sort of "bad apple".

Hold on a second-
[Bad Apple plays in horrible quality]

Yes, I did also add sound to the runtime. 
The address that you've selected when you

run the dot operator would normally be 
used to specify which character to print,

but here it specifies a MIDI note to play 
for the rest of the frame. In my game,

I added basic blip sound effects 
for picking up an apple, and a

3-note sequence for when the player dies. 
I think that's about as good as it gets.
Oh, and for the sake of completeness,

I did also write a BF interpreter in 
Portal 2. It just... felt wrong not to.

Anyway, if you enjoyed this rabbithole, 
you can subscribe for more unhinged

sidequests. And just in case you 
still have any doubts about this,

here's the first frame of the game being 
run in a vanilla BF interpreter. There's

no graphical output of course, but the memory 
buffer does line up with the expected pixels.

Alright, for real this time, 
that's all from me today- see ya!
